coredata.dc:title: Application of maleimide modified graphene quantum dots and porphyrin fluorescence resonance energy transfer in the design of ‘‘turn-on’’ fluorescence sensors for biothiols
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Gao
Yu, Song
Qiang, Zhang
Xiuying, Liu
Tang lijun,
Jiangrong, Li
coredata.dc:description: Two novel fluorescent probes were designed to detect the biothiol in foods using the highly efficient Michael addition reaction between maleimide-derived probes and the biothiol. First, maleimide functionalized GQDs (M-GQDs) were synthesized and used for biothiol identification according to the Michael addition principle. The biothiol can be detected in the range of 5 × 10−9 to 4 × 10−7 mol/L and the detection limit was 1.69 × 10−9 mol/L. Then, a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system between M-GQDs and tetrakis (4-aminophenyl) porphyrin (TAPP) for biothiol detection was developed. However, the process of FRET was switched off in the presence of biothiols due to the switch of M-GQDs fluorescence emission to the“ON” mode following the Michael addition mechanism. The system could quickly and accurately detect the biothiol with a detection range of 6.7 × 10−10 to 2 × 10−7 mol/L and a detection limit of 2.34 × 10−10 mol/L. Compared to the single detection system, the FRET system had a wider detection range and lower detection limit, and the related biomolecules did not interfere with the quantitative identification of the biothiol. The proposed method was successfully applied for the determination of the biothiol in foods and human blood samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Porphyrin
FRET
Fluorescence “turn-on”
Biothiols

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of maleimide-functionalized graphene quantum dots (M-GQDs)
Sensing procedures for the biothiols (GSH and Cys)
Detection of biothiols in actual samples
Quenching effect of 4-maleimidobenzoic acid on the fluorescence of GQDs
Fluorescence detection of biothiol with M-GQDs complex
FRET-based compound of M-GQDs and TAPP
Detection of biothiol based on FRET system
Analysis of GSH in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence intensity of GQDs with the addition of different maleimide concentrations (A–F: maleimide concentrations are 0, 1 × 10−8, 1 × 10−7, 1 × 10−6, 1 × 10−5, 1 × 10−4 mol/L), the concentration of GQDs was 0.28 mg/mL; (B) the correlation curve between I/I0 (I and I0 were the fluorescence intensities of GQDs before and after the addition of maleimide) and maleimide concentration.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of M-GQDs with the addition of different GSH concentrations (A–F: GSH concentrations are 0, 5 × 10−9, 1 × 10−8, 5 × 10−8, 2 × 10−7, 4 × 10−7 mol/L); (B) the correlation curve between I/I0 (I and I0 were the fluorescence intensities of GQDs before and after the addition of GSH) GSH concentration.
Fluorescence emission spectra of M-GQDs alone, and in the presence of Cys or GSH.
Absorption and fluorescence spectra of normalized M-GQDs and TAPP solutions.
Fluorescence spectra of the FRET system with the addition of different concentrations of TAPP (A–F: TAPP concentrations are 0, 6.67 × 10−6, 1.33 × 10−5, 2 × 10−5, 2.67 × 10−5, 3.33 × 10−5 mol/L).

Fluorescence decay curves for GQDs with or without TAPP. Red lines: in the absence of TAPP. Green lines: in the presence of TAPP (3.33 × 10−5 mol/L). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of TAPP in the presence of different concentrations of GSH (A–F: GSH concentrations are 0, 6.7 × 10−10,3.3 × 10−9, 6.7 × 10−9, 3.3 × 10−8, 6.7 × 10−8, 2 × 10−7 mol/L); (B) The correlation curve between ITAPP/IGQDs (ITAPP and IQDs represented the fluorescence intensities of TAPP and GQDs) and GSH concentration.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of TAPP in the presence of different concentrations of Cys (A–F: Cys concentrations are 0, 3.33 × 10−9, 6.67 × 10−9, 3.33 × 10−8, 6.67 × 10−8, 1 × 10−7 mol/L); (B) the correlation curve between ITAPP/IGQDs (ITAPP and IGQDs represented the fluorescence intensities of TAPP and GQDs) and Cys concentration.
(A) Fluorescence quenching mechanism of M-GQDs. (B) Detection mechanism of biothiol based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer system.
Interference of coexisting substances in GSH detection (3.3 × 10−8 mol/L).
Determination of GSH in fruit samples.
Detection of GSH in human serum.

head.ce:title: Application of maleimide modified graphene quantum dots and porphyrin fluorescence resonance energy transfer in the design of ‘‘turn-on’’ fluorescence sensors for biothiols

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Bohai University, National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Storage, Processing and Safety Control Technology for Fresh Agricultural and Aquatic Products, Food Safety Key Lab of Liaoning Province, The Fresh Food Storage and Processing Technology Research Institute of Liaoning Provincial Universities, Jinzhou, Liaoning, 121013, China

Processing and Safety Control Technology for Fresh Agricultural and Aquatic Products
Food Safety Key Lab of Liaoning Province
The Fresh Food Storage and Processing Technology Research Institute of Liaoning Provincial Universities

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Technology, Bohai University, National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Storage, Processing and Safety Control Technology for Fresh Agricultural and Aquatic Products, Food Safety Key Lab of Liaoning Province, The Fresh Food Storage and Processing Technology Research Institute of Liaoning Provincial Universities, Jinzhou, Liaoning, 121013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Mathematics and Mathematics, Jinzhou, Liaoning, 121013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Bohai University, Jinzhou, Liaoning, 121013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Bohai University, Jinzhou, Liaoning, 121013, People’s China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two novel fluorescent probes were designed to detect the biothiol in foods using the highly efficient Michael addition reaction between maleimide-derived probes and the biothiol. First, maleimide functionalized GQDs (M-GQDs) were synthesized and used for biothiol identification according to the Michael addition principle. The biothiol can be detected in the range of 5 × 10−9 to 4 × 10−7 mol/L and the detection limit was 1.69 × 10−9 mol/L. Then, a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system between M-GQDs and tetrakis (4-aminophenyl) porphyrin (TAPP) for biothiol detection was developed. However, the process of FRET was switched off in the presence of biothiols due to the switch of M-GQDs fluorescence emission to the“ON” mode following the Michael addition mechanism. The system could quickly and accurately detect the biothiol with a detection range of 6.7 × 10−10 to 2 × 10−7 mol/L and a detection limit of 2.34 × 10−10 mol/L. Compared to the single detection system, the FRET system had a wider detection range and lower detection limit, and the related biomolecules did not interfere with the quantitative identification of the biothiol. The proposed method was successfully applied for the determination of the biothiol in foods and human blood samples.

ce:section.ce:para: As a newly developed and discovered member in graphene family, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), are zero-dimensional nanomaterials which possess lower toxicity than that of two-dimensional graphene derivatives [1]. Taking the advantages of unique optical properties, lower toxicity and biocompatibility, GQDs are one of the emerging and promising optical nanomaterials for prospective applications in chemical sensors [2], photovoltaics [3], bio-imaging [4], and bio/sensors [5–7]. The tunable photoluminescence (PL) and exceptional photostability of GQDs enable them as outstanding bioimaging agents for tracking therapeutic responses. GQDs are as promising as PL sensors with regard to the simple functionalization and strong luminescence [8]. Numerous studies have reported the development of high-sensitivity detecting biomolecules using GQDs as biosensors [9]. GQDs with appropriate surface functionalization can be used to detect biological and chemical molecules due to their ultrahigh specific surface area [10].

Biological thiols such as glutathione (GSH), homocysteine (Hcy), and cysteine (Cys) play important roles in biological systems [11–13]. Specifically, Cys and Hcy participate in cellular growth, and GSH is a key factor that is responsible for redox homeostasis. Therefore, quantitative assay of biothiols in biological fluids is the great value in biochemistry and clinical chemistry. It has been reported that the people can supplement biothiols by eating fresh fruits and vegetables which are important sources of biothiols. Therefore, quantifying and comparing biothiols in the different foods are essential to refer to the number of supplements required. Currently, the most commonly used assay for detecting these biological thiols are high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [14], electrochemical assays [15], enzymatic methods [16], fluorescent detection using synthetic receptors [17] and quantum dots [18]. Highly sensitive and selective fluorescent probes have been developed for detecting biological thiols, however, their applications are limited as a result of complicated synthesis procedure [19–21]. Thus, fluorescence nanoprobes are produced on the basis on various mechanisms of biothiols detection [22]. A large number of fluorescent probes are developed for the selective recognition of the biothiol through the Michael addition [23], thiazolidine formation [24], or cyclization reactions with aldehydes [25], and others [26]. Due to the easy response between thiols and Michael acceptor, the fluorophores functionalized with maleimide have been successfully utilized for biothiols sensing [22].

In this paper, we designed two sensing mechanisms to detect biothiols. One mechanism was the formation of maleimide functionalized GQDs (M-GQDs) fluorescent probes for biothiols detection via the Michael addition mechanism (Scheme 1 A). The M-GQDs fluorescence was quenched by the linking of maleimide, and the fluorescence of M-GQDs will be restored (“turned ON”) in the presence of biothiols. The other mechanism was the construction of the FRET system between M-GQDs and TAPP for the biothiol detection. Among various fluorophores, porphyrins have been used in the development of chemical sensors due to their high molar absorption coefficients and long fluorescence emission wavelengths, making them promising candidates in biological systems applications. However, limited researches have been reported on porphyrins-based probes for biothiols. As M-GQDs possess negative charges (due to the presence of carboxylate group on the surface), and porphyrin molecules possess positive charges (due to the presence of amino group on the surface), the negatively charged M-GQDs and positively charged porphyrin molecules are in close proximity through electrostatic interaction and π-π stacking. The fluorescence quenching of M-GQDs in the presence of porphyrin indicated the FRET from M-GQDs to porphyrin. The fluorescence of M-GQDs was “turned ON” through their interactions with biothiols via Michael addition mechanism (Scheme 1B). By comparison, we found that the FRET system had a wider detection range and lower detection limit.

All the reagents were analytical grade and used as obtained without further purification. Graphite powder with the metal’s basis of 99.95% (325 mech), Phosphorus pentoxide (P2O5, ≥98.0%), Tetrakis (4-aminophenyl) porphyrin, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Potassium peroxydisulfate (K2S2O8) was obtained from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) and Hydrogen peroxide (30%) were supplied by Tianli Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). Concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%) was acquired from Jinzhou Chemical Reagent Company. 4-Maleimidobenzoic acid was purchased in Fluoro Chem Company. Cysteine (Cys) and reduced glutathione (GSH) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works. The fruit samples were bought from a local grocery store in our university. Water with a resistivity ≥18 MΩ/cm was used in all experiments. A Fluoromax-4NIR spectra fluorophotometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, NJ, USA) equipped with quartz cuvette (1 cm path-length) was used to conduct fluorescence measurements of samples. pH values were recorded using a PHS-3C pH meter (Tuopu Co., Hangzhou, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of maleimide-functionalized graphene quantum dots (M-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared using the Hummers’ method with slightly modification as previously reported [27]. GQDs were further prepared using GO through one-step solvothermal method. First, 5 mL (0.1 mg/mL) GO was mixed with 3 mL ammonia water (28%) and 5 mL deionized water, and stirred for 1 h. Then, the mixture was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (50 mL). After reacting at 170 °C for 5 h, the obtained mixture was cooled to room temperature naturally and was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, and the supernatant was refluxed at 100 °C for 1.5 h. The obtained solution was GQDs modified with amino group. The concentration of GQDs was 1.68 mg/mL.

M-GQDs preparation was described briefly (Scheme 1A): 0.01 g EDC and 0.005 g NHS dissolved in 100 μL PBS was added into 800 μL 4-maleimidobenzoic acid solution with different concentrations, respectively. 4-Maleimidobenzoic acid was activated through the magnetically stirring of the mixture at room temperature for 30 min. After that, the activated 4-maleimidobenzoic acid with the addition of 500 μL GQDs solution (0.28 mg/mL) were incubated at room temperature for 4 h to obtain M-GQDs conjugates. Then the fluorescence spectra were detected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing procedures for the biothiols (GSH and Cys)
ce:section.ce:para: The different concentrations of biothiols (GSH and Cys) were added to the M-GQDs complex, and PBS (pH = 7.4) was used to supplement the volume to 3 ml. After incubating at room temperature for 30 min, the fluorescence spectra were measured under 400 nm excitation.

Known amounts of M-GQDs and TAPP were added into 2 mL centrifuge tubes containing various biothiols concentrations (GSH and Cys). The mixture was then diluted to 2.0 mL with PBS (pH 7.4) and agitated at room temperature for 30 min. Then, the fluorescence emission spectra over 420–780 nm with an excitation of 400 nm were recorded. The calibration curve for ITAPP/IM-GQDs versus melamine concentration was established (where ITAPP and IM-GQDs referred to the fluorescence intensities of TAPP and M-GQDs, respectively).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of biothiols in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: In the actual sample detection, fruit samples were first washed and dried naturally, followed by the homogenization using a mixer. The pulp and supernatant were separated by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was then passed through a 0.45 μm filter and diluted to an appropriate concentration within the detection range by ultrapure water [28]. For real sample analysis, the same pretreatment was applied to biothiols (known concentration) spiked fruit sample.

The fresh human blood samples provided by the local hospital were used to obtain human serum samples by centrifugation of fresh human blood at 1000 rpm for 5 min. Then, the serum samples were diluted for 10 times using PBS for protein detection as a pretreatment. The same pretreatment was applied to melamine (known concentration) spiked milk sample.

One-step solvothermal method was used to prepare GQDs with amino functionality. As shown in Fig. S1, The GQDs were an overall quasi spherical morphology with particles size ranging from 1 to 3 nm, with an average diameter of ca 2.3 nm. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of GQDs was shown in Fig. S2a. The results showed that GQDs contained hydrophilic functional groups of amino group, carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy groups, leading to rapid dissolve in water and excellent water solubility that were helpful for subsequent experiments. All GQDs exhibited the excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior, where the PL intensity significantly decreased with the PL peak shift from 491 to 521 nm during the excitation wavelength from 400 to 470 nm (Fig. S3). The fluorescent carbon nanomaterial showed extensive excitation-dependent PL behavior owing to optical choose of GQDs with different size distribution and surface defects [29–31].

GQDs functionalized with maleimide groups (M-GQDs) were accomplished through the covalent amidation response between the –NH2 groups of the GQDs and –COOH groups of the maleimide compound, respectively, via EDC/NHS chemistry (Scheme 1A). As shown in Fig. S2, GQDs exhibited characteristic peaks of phenyl hydroxyl group at 3430 cm−1, amino group at 3128 cm−1 and carbon-carbon double bonds at 1639 cm−1. Maleimide benzoic acid (Fig. S2b) had a stretching vibration peak of carbon-oxygen double bond at 1716 cm−1. By comparing with GQDs, M-GQDs (Fig. S2c) also had a phenyl hydroxyl stretching vibration absorption peak at 3134 cm−1, and a characteristic secondary primary amide bond peak appeared at 1652 cm−1 and 1734 cm−1, which indicated that M-GQDs complex had been successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching effect of 4-maleimidobenzoic acid on the fluorescence of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The intensive quenching effect of 4-maleimidobenzoic acid on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs was observed at 400 nm in Fig. 1 . The fluorescence intensity of GQDs gradually decreased with the addition of 4-maleimidobenzoic acid to the solution. Maleimide groups can effectively quench the fluorescence of GQDs because their low n-π∗ nonradiative effect promotes the decay of excited states [32]. After the maleimide was connected with the electron-rich substance or donor fluorescence group, the electrons were transferred from the fluorescence group to the electron-deficient maleimide ring, which further quenched or weakened the fluorescence emission of donor molecules. Fig. 1b showed a fine linear relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity I/I0 (I and I0 referred to the fluorescence intensity of GQDs with or without maleimide, respectively) and the logarithm of the concentration of the maleimide (CMaleimide) within the range of 1.0 × 10−8 - 1.0 × 10−4 mol/L following the linear regression equation of I/I0 = 0.69–0.03logCMaleimide (R2 = 0.999) (Fig. 1b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of biothiol with M-GQDs complex
FRET-based compound of M-GQDs and TAPP
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption and emission spectra of GQDs and TAPP were shown in Fig. 4 . The emission spectra of GQDs overlapped obviously with the absorption spectra of TAPP, indicating the energy transfer from GQDs and TAPP. On the contrary, it was impossible to transfer energy from TAPP to GQDs, mainly because of the inconsistency of energy between them, and the lack of overlap between the absorption spectrum of GQDs and the emission spectrum of TAPP. Consequently, 400 nm excitation light was chosen to excite the donor, ensuring the well stimulation of GQDs and no stimulation of TAPP, which effectively reduced the interference caused by direct excitation of the receptor.

As shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensity of GQDs gradually decreased with the increase of TAPP concentration while the fluorescence intensity of TAPP showed the opposite trend, and there was an obvious energy transfer between the two substances. The fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) was caused by the π-π stacking and electrostatic reaction between M-GQDs and TAPP, in which M-GQDs were negatively charged (because their surfaces were modified by carboxyl groups) and TAPP was positively charged (TAPP had amino groups). Under the action of electrostatic force, the two substances attracted each other through the connection of maleimide bonds and interacted to form a new complex (Fig. 5). The electron absorption energy in the first orbit transited to a higher orbit, which resulted in the change of PL intensity in the Q band of TAPP molecule. In these assemblies, the GQDs acted as the photon absorption antenna. Energy was passed from the GQDs donor to TAPP acceptor by the FRET mechanism.

We know that the lifetime properties of the FRET donor would alter because of nonradiative exciton transfer [34,35]. To further prove the occurrence of energy transfer from M-GQDs (donor) to TAPP (acceptor), the decay curves for the M-GQDs before and after adding TAPP had been recorded as shown in Fig. 6 . In addition, the average lifetimes for the M-GQDs with or without TAPP were calculated by fitting the data to a three exponential decay curve, from which the average lifetime decreased from 3.93 ns to 2.79 ns after added 3.33 × 10−5 mol/L of TAPP into the system. The decrease in fluorescence emission lifetime provided additional evidence that the FRET process was passed from the M-GQDs donor to TAPP acceptor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of biothiol based on FRET system
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence of GQDs recovered significantly after adding GSH to FRET system, while the fluorescence intensity of TAPP decreased continuously. There were two main reasons for this phenomenon. Firstly, the whole system was linked by maleimide group, which had specificity and strong affinity to preferentially bind –SH of biothiol and form saturated maleimide bonds through Michael addition reaction, further resulting in the drop of GQDs originally attached to it. Therefore, the energy transfer was interrupted and GQDs were converted to fluorescence “ON” mode. Secondly, it was proposed that –SH could act as a spacer between GQDs and TAPP, leading to the destruction of the π-π interaction and the disintegration of the “donor-receptor” relationship between GQDs and TAPP. Therefore, the fluorescence of GQDs was “ON” (Fig. 7). Fig. 7 illustrated plots of the fluorescence intensity ratio (ITAPP/IGQDs) versus the concentration of GSH. A good linearity was observed in the GSH concentration range of 6.7 × 10−10 -1.3 × 10−7 mol/L. A limit of detection (LOD) of 2.34 × 10−10 mol/L was calculated based on three times of standard deviation of the blank signal (S/N = 3) for GSH. The linear regression equation was ITAPP/IGQDs = -1.088-0.16logCGSH (mol/L) with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.999.

Similarly, another biothiol Cys can also be detected by the sensor. The fluorescence spectra of FRET with different concentrations of Cys, the standard curve between the fluorescence intensity ratio of the two substances and the concentration of Cys were shown in Fig. 8 . The Cys concentration showed a linear relationship with the fluorescence intensity ratio ITAPP/IGQDs within the Cys concentration range of 3.33 × 10−9 - 1.0 × 10−7 mol/L. The linear regression equation was ITAPP/IGQDs = -0.75-0.18logCCys (mol/L) with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.997. The limit of detection (LOD) was 1.3 × 10−9 mol/L.

By comparing the two fluorescence probes, no obvious change was observed in the fluorescence recovery effect of the single M-GQDs system, while the fluorescence resonance energy transfer system showed better fluorescence recovery effect. Moreover, wider detection range of 6.7 × 10−10 - 1.3 × 10−7 mol/L and lower minimum detection limit of 1.3 × 10−7 mol/L were obtained when using fluorescence energy transfer system as a probe to detect GSH compared to the use of M-GQDs, which had the detection range of 5.0 × 10−9 - 4.0 × 10−7 mol/L, and the minimum detection limit of 1.69 × 10−9 mol/L. Therefore, the FRET system was more preferable for the detection of the biothiol.

Usually, some coexisting substances may affect the detection of actual samples. In order to explore the precision of this method, solutions with the GSH concentration of 3.3 × 10−8 mol/L and different potential sources of interferences were tested under the same conditions. Table 1 was the determination of interference effects of inorganic metal ions, glucose, BSA, trypsin and other amino acids. The relative error was less than (±5.0%) which proved that the substance had no interference in the detection of the biothiol. From Table 1, the coexisting substances with 3.0 × 10−8 mol/L GSH was over 104-fold for NaCl, KCl, CaCl2 and Mg (NO3)2, 106-fold for glucose and glycine, 103-fold for BSA, 105-fold for trypsin, alanine and tryptophan. The experimental results showed that these coexisting substances had little interference with the detection of GSH. Therefore, this method was proved to have high selectivity for detecting the GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of GSH in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The FRET system was used to detect GSH in three different fruit samples to verify the applicability of this method in the actual sample detection. In conventional detection techniques, the GSH needed complex processing steps and extraction procedures, which were time- and cost-consuming and liable to pesticide loss. In our experiment, the pretreatment as preciously described in Experimental section was applied to fruit samples, avoiding the use of organic solvents and tedious preconcentration steps. In the actual detection, the processed fruit sample solution was diluted for testing until the desired concentration was achieved. As shown in Table 2 , the detection recoveries of GSH in cucumber were 90%–106%, with the relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) less than 1.4%. The detection recoveries of GSH in orange was 95%–103%, with the RSD less than 2.0%. The detection recoveries of GSH in apple was 93%–103%, with the RSD less than 2.1%. Results showed that this method had practical application value in the detection of GSH in actual samples. Compared with other detection methods, the results were reliable and accurate.

Because the human body usually obtains the biothiol through its own metabolism and food intake. The human serum used to detect the GSH content in it was obtained by simple treatment of human blood. As shown in Table 3 , the detection recoveries of GSH in human serum were 98%–106%, with the RSD less than 1.1%. The experimental results showed that the fluorescent sensor could not only detect the content of the biothiol in food, but also was applicable in the biothiol detection in the blood. This will be helpful and influential for the future detection of the biothiol in medicine and various forms of food.

Two kinds of fluorescent probes were designed based on maleimide-functionalized GQDs (M-GQDs). First, M-GQDs were formed using GQDs and maleimide benzoic acid. Due to the Michael addition reaction between maleimide and thiol, the biothiol can be detected based on the fluorescence quenching of GQDs. Then, a rapid, sensitive and selective method for the detection of the biothiol was developed using the FRET between M-GQDs and TAPP as “ON” fluorescence sensors. In this system, the addition of the biothiol inhibited the FRET between M-GQDs and TAPP, resulting in the decreased fluorescence intensity of TAPP and the increased fluorescence intensity of M-GQDs. Under the optimum conditions, the biothiol could be detected based on the fluorescence intensity ratio ITAPP/IGQDs in the FRET system. Therefore, this method can successfully be used for the detection of biothiol in foods and human blood samples.

Gao Xue: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Writing - original draft. Song Yu: Data curation, Formal analysis. Zhang Qiang: Data curation, Validation. Liu Xiuying: Writing - review & editing. Tang lijun: Writing - review & editing. Li Jiangrong: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by a grant from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31501564), the central government guides local science and technology development special funds project (No. 2018107005), the Research Project from Science & Technology Department of Liaoning Province of China (N0. 2015103020), the Team Support Program for the Taishan Scholar of Blue Industry leading personnel of shandong Province of China (LZBZ2015-19).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2020.01.062.

M. Nurunnabi, Z. Khatun, G.R. Reeck, D.Y. Lee, Y. Lee, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 6 (2014) 12413-12421.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: D. I. Son, B. Kwon, W.D.H. Park, W.S. Seo, Y. Yi, B. Angadi, C.L. Lee, W.K. Choi, 7 (2012) 465-471.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.J. Liu, X.L. Zhang, Z.X. Cong, Z.T. Chen, H.H. Yang, G.N. Chen, Nanoscale. 5 (2013) 1810-1815.
